1: ʾělōhay ʾattâ — "you are my God" — the intimate possessive framing the entire psalm. ʾēśāʾ ʾĕrômmĕmkā — "I will exalt you." The basis: YHWH did wondrous things (pālaʾ ʿēṣôt mērāḥôq) — "wonderful counsels from afar," i.e., plans laid long ago now fulfilled. ʿēṣôt (plans/counsels) is the same word as 14:26-27 — YHWH's sovereign governance of history proved by events. ʾĕmûnâ ʾōmen — "faithfulness/firmness of old/truth."
2: śamtā mēʿîr lĕgal — "you have made a city into a heap of rubble" (gal, a heap of stones); mibṣār lĕmappēlâ — "a fortified city into a ruin." The unidentified city (probably representative of all hostile powers rather than a specific city) is contrasted with YHWH's eternal city Zion. The city of foreigners (qiryat zārîm) will never be rebuilt (lōʾ tîbbāneh lĕʿôlām).
3: ʿam ʿāz — "a strong/fierce people" will honor YHWH; qiryat gôyîm ʿārîṣîm — "city of ruthless/terrible nations" will fear him. The reversal: the nations that trusted in military strength now honor YHWH. This is the eschatological conversion of the gentile nations — not their destruction but their submission and worship, which is the hope of vv.6-8.
4: kî-hāyîtā māʿôz laddāl māʿôz lāʾebyôn — "you have been a stronghold to the poor/needy" (dāl + ʾebyôn, the two key Hebrew terms for the economically marginal). maḥăseh mizzārem — "refuge from the storm." The image of YHWH as shelter specifically for the vulnerable is one of Isaiah's persistent themes (cf. 14:30; 32:2) and a defining characteristic of his eschatological king.
5: kĕḥōreb bĕṣāyôn — "like heat in a dry place" — the powerful nations are reduced like excessive heat moderated by a cloud's shadow (ṣēl ʿāb). zammîr ʿārîṣîm yaʿăneh — "the song of the terrible ones is silenced/humbled" — the victory songs of the conquering nations go quiet before YHWH's intervention. The powerful are not merely defeated but shamed into silence.
6: mišteh šĕmānîm — "feast of fat things/rich food"; mišteh šĕmārîm — "feast of well-aged wine" (šĕmārîm, from šāmar, to keep/preserve — aged wine kept on its dregs). The eschatological banquet on Mount Zion is for kol-hāʿammîm — "all peoples" — not Israel alone. The double qualification šĕmānîm mĕmullāḥîm ("marrow-rich"), šĕmārîm mĕzuqqāqîm ("well-refined") emphasizes the superlative quality of what YHWH provides.
7: hallôṭ hallûṭ — "the covering that is spread/cast" (lûṭ, H3875, wrapped/veiled); hamsĕkâ hamsûkâ — "the veil that is woven over" (māsak, to weave/cover). The double image of a shroud/veil covering all peoples is the metaphor for death and mourning that YHWH removes. The removal is not merely comforting but cosmic — death as a regime is dismantled.
8: billaʿ hammāwet lāneṣaḥ — "he will swallow up death forever" (bālaʿ, H1104, to swallow, consume). Paul quotes this verse in 1 Cor 15:54 at the climax of his resurrection argument: "Death is swallowed up in victory." The word lāneṣaḥ (forever/in victory — the same word the NT reads as "victory") makes the swallowing definitive and permanent. māḥâ ʾădōnāy Yhwh dimʿâ mēʿal kol-pānîm — "Lord YHWH will wipe away tears from all faces" — quoted directly in Rev 21:4.
9: The response of the saved remnant: hinnēh ʾĕlōhênû zeh — "behold, this is our God" — the climactic identification of the God who acts is made in the moment of salvation. qiwwînû lô — "we waited for him/hoped in him"; yôšîʿēnû — "he will save us." The waiting (qāwâ, H6960) is the posture that characterizes faith in Isaiah's theology (cf. 8:17; 30:18; 40:31).
10: kî tānûaḥ yad Yhwh bāhar hazzeh — "for the hand of YHWH will rest on this mountain" — Mount Zion as the place of YHWH's settled presence. The contrast: Moab is trampled (yûddaš) like straw in a dunghill (mô madmēnâ) — the trampling imagery for judgment. pāraś yādāyw bĕqirbô — "he spreads his hands in the midst of it" like a swimmer spreading his arms to swim.
11: The swimmer comparison: one who swims extends his arms (yiśḥeh lišĕḥôt) — so YHWH humbles (šippal) Moab's pride (gāʾôn) and the skill/craft of his hands (ʿim miśgab yādāyw). The precise mechanism of humiliation — the very extension of hands that is skillful swimming becomes the gesture of defeat — turns Moab's effort into the means of its own downfall.
12: ûmiḇṣar māʿûzzê ḥômōtêkā — "the fortress and the high walls of your fortifications" — Moab's military security. higgîaʿ hišpîl hĭggiyaʿ lāʾāreṣ — "he brings them low, he brings them down to the ground" — the threefold verb descent mirrors the cosmic descent imagery of 14:11-15. Even the most fortified human security has no defense against YHWH's cosmic judgment.
